title: Perseverance Scratches the Martian Surface, Finds Organic Carbon
gist: NASA's Perseverance rover detected macromolecular organic carbon in the Bright Angel mudstones of Jezero Crater (per Science Advances) using its SHERLOC instrument — another tantalizing but non-conclusive hint that life may have existed on Mars.

insights.claim: NASA's Perseverance rover detected macromolecular carbon (MMC) in the Bright Angel mudstones of Jezero Crater using its SHERLOC ultraviolet-laser spectroscopy instrument, per a Science Advances paper.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Continued organic-chemistry detection on Mars sustains the scientific case for sample-return and crewed missions.
insights.locator: Using an onboard instrument called SHERLOC

insights.claim: The detected macromolecular carbon can be generated by living organisms but also by non-living sources like meteorites, so the find is suggestive of past Martian life but not conclusive.
insights.speaker: Jake Currie
insights.so_what: Ambiguity keeps the life-on-Mars question open, motivating future return missions.
insights.quote: This large, resilient form of carbon can be generated by living organisms, but it can come from non-living sources (like meteorites) as well.
insights.locator: planetary scientists led by Ashley Murphy
